title: UNICEF Youth Development Internship in Qatar 2026: Fully Explained Opportunity for Young Qatari Professionals
summary: UNICEF is offering an exciting opportunity for young graduates and students through its Youth Development Intern – Gulf Area Office programme based in Qatar. This prestigious three-month internship is designed for passionate young people who want to contribute to youth empowerment, peacebuilding, adolescent development, and social impact initiatives while gaining hands-on experience with one of the world’s leading humanitarian organizations. The internship provides successful candidates with exposure to international development work, partnerships with government institutions, youth-focused programming, and policy discussions that shape the future of children and adolescents across the Gulf region. For individuals interested in development work, youth engagement, social policy, education, and international cooperation, this internship offers an exceptional platform to gain practical experience while contributing to meaningful change. About UNICEF UNICEF operates in more than 190 countries and territories worldwide, working to protect children’s rights, improve access to education and healthcare, strengthen child protection systems, and support vulnerable communities. The organization works across various sectors including: Education Child protection Youth empowerment Health and nutrition Emergency response Social policy Adolescent development Data and research UNICEF’s mission focuses on ensuring every child has access to safety, dignity, opportunity, and a better future. Background of UNICEF’s Gulf Area Office in Qatar In 2018, UNICEF signed a Basic Cooperation Agreement (BCA) with the State of Qatar, leading to the establishment of the UNICEF Qatar Office in 2019. Since then, the office has expanded rapidly and strengthened collaboration with national institutions and regional stakeholders. The Qatar Office is part of UNICEF’s broader Gulf Area subregional programme, which includes: Bahrain Kuwait Saudi Arabia United Arab Emirates Qatar The Gulf Area Office focuses on several strategic priorities, including: Early childhood development Child protection Youth participation and empowerment Evidence and data systems for children Strengthening partnerships for child welfare This internship gives participants direct exposure to these regional initiatives. Overview of the UNICEF Youth Development Internship The internship programme was created to help undergraduate, graduate, and postgraduate students gain practical experience in professional environments aligned with their academic training. Interns will work under the supervision of the Youth and Adolescent Development Manager and support UNICEF’s youth-related programmes and partnerships in Qatar. Internship Duration Three months Internship Location Qatar Department Gulf Area Office Youth and Adolescent Development Programme Key Responsibilities of the Intern Selected interns will support several important projects and initiatives related to youth development and peacebuilding. 1. Support Youth Participation and Engagement Activities Interns will assist in programmes that encourage: Youth leadership Civic participation Community engagement Adolescent empowerment Youth inclusion initiatives This role allows interns to contribute to activities that amplify youth voices and participation in national development. 2. Assist Youth Skills Development Programmes The intern will contribute to initiatives that support: Skills training Career readiness Transition-to-employment pathways Youth empowerment projects Capacity-building activities This area focuses on helping young people develop employable skills and improve economic opportunities. 3. Contribute to the Arab Forum on Youth, Peace and Security One of the internship’s major highlights is supporting preparatory activities for the Arab Forum on Youth, Peace and Security (YPS) . Responsibilities may include: Research support Coordination activities Background documentation Stakeholder communication Event preparation This provides valuable exposure to regional peacebuilding and policy discussions. 4. Coordinate With Government and Local Partners Interns will work closely with: Ministry of Sports and Youth Local organizations UN agencies Youth-focused partners Tasks include: Following up on programme activities Supporting partnership coordination Monitoring implementation progress Assisting communication among stakeholders 5. Develop Terms of Reference (TORs) Interns may support the preparation and monitoring of: Project TORs Youth initiative documentation Consultation materials Programme planning documents This helps participants gain practical experience in programme management and development-sector operations. 6. Participate in Meetings and Documentation Interns will attend and document meetings involving: UNICEF programme teams Government ministries UN agencies Youth organizations Responsibilities include: Recording proceedings Preparing summaries Tracking action points Supporting programme coordination 7. Support Monitoring and Reporting Interns will contribute to: Programme tracking Monitoring implementation progress Drafting reports Preparing updates Supporting evaluation activities This offers experience in results-based programme management and reporting. Benefits of the UNICEF Internship This internship offers several important professional and personal development benefits. Practical International Development Experience Interns gain first-hand experience working with a globally respected humanitarian organization. Professional Networking Opportunities Participants interact with: UNICEF professionals Government stakeholders Youth leaders International development experts UN agency representatives Skills Development Interns strengthen skills in: Communication Research Project coordination Partnership management Reporting and documentation Policy engagement Exposure to Regional Youth Programmes Interns learn about major Gulf-region initiatives related to: Youth peace and security Child protection Education Social development Career Advancement Opportunities Experience with UNICEF can significantly strengthen future applications for: International organizations NGOs Government programmes Fellowships Graduate opportunities Eligibility Requirements Applicants must meet all of the following criteria. Academic Qualifications Applicants must: Be currently enrolled in an undergraduate, graduate, or PhD programme OR Have graduated within the past two years Accepted fields include: Education Economics Social Sciences Related disciplines Age Requirement Applicants must be: At least 18 years old Language Requirements Applicants must demonstrate: Good written English Good spoken English Good written Arabic Good spoken Arabic Bilingual communication skills are essential for this role. Knowledge and Experience Candidates should have: Familiarity with youth and adolescent programmes Interest in youth development Understanding of social development issues Strong communication and coordination abilities Nationality Requirement Applicants must be: Qatari nationals This internship opportunity is specifically reserved for citizens of Qatar. UNICEF Core Values and Competencies UNICEF expects interns to demonstrate its CRITAS values: Care Respect Integrity Trust Accountability Sustainability The organization also seeks candidates who can: Build partnerships Think strategically Work collaboratively Innovate and adapt Deliver results Demonstrate ethical awareness UNICEF’s Commitment to Diversity and Inclusion UNICEF promotes equal opportunity and welcomes applications from individuals regardless of: Gender Ethnicity Religion Disability status Socio-economic background Sexual orientation The organization also provides reasonable accommodation for persons with disabilities throughout the recruitment process. Important Recruitment Information Applicants should note the following: UNICEF does not charge any application fee Only shortlisted candidates will be contacted Background checks will be conducted Academic credentials may be verified Medical clearance may be required Vaccination requirements may apply Applicants are advised to apply only through official UNICEF recruitment platforms. Deadline for Application The official deadline was not stated in the provided announcement. Interested applicants are encouraged to apply as early as possible before the position closes. Click here for more and to apply. Why You Should Apply for This UNICEF Internship This internship is more than a temporary placement. It is an opportunity to: Work with a globally recognized UN organization Gain exposure to international development work Build professional experience in youth programming Develop leadership and policy engagement skills Contribute to meaningful social impact initiatives For young Qatari students and graduates passionate about youth development, peacebuilding, and international cooperation, this programme offers an excellent pathway into the development sector. Click here for more global internship opportunities The post UNICEF Youth Development Internship in Qatar 2026: Fully Explained Opportunity for Young Qatari Professionals appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Funded PhD Studentship in Generative AI and Climate Change Misinformation at UCD 2026
summary: Applications are now open for a fully funded four-year PhD studentship focused on Generative AI and Climate Change Misinformation at University College Dublin (UCD) . This research opportunity is part of a wider multi-institutional, interdisciplinary national centre for data science and artificial intelligence funded by Research Ireland . The studentship will explore one of the most urgent questions of the digital age: how generative AI tools such as ChatGPT, Gemini, and other large language models may influence public understanding, beliefs, and behaviours around climate change. As more people use AI chatbots to search for information about complex topics, the reliability, accuracy, and persuasive power of AI-generated content have become important areas of academic, social, and environmental concern. The successful applicant will begin the programme in September 2026 and will join a structured, cohort-based PhD training programme that combines individual research with advanced interdisciplinary training, peer learning, professional development, and career-focused support. Supervisors The PhD project will be supervised by: Prof. Ciara Greene , UCD School of Psychology Prof. Andrew Parnell , UCD School of Mathematics and Statistics The project brings together psychology, statistics, artificial intelligence, misinformation studies, behavioural science, climate communication, and data science. This makes it especially suitable for candidates interested in understanding how emerging technologies shape public opinion and decision-making. About the PhD Studentship This funded PhD studentship will investigate the relationship between generative AI and climate misinformation. The project focuses on how large language models respond to climate-related questions, how they may reproduce or challenge misinformation, and how exposure to AI-generated climate content may influence users’ beliefs and behaviours. Generative AI chatbots are increasingly used as information sources. People ask them questions about science, politics, health, education, finance, and climate change. However, these systems are trained on large datasets that may contain both reliable information and misleading content. This creates an important research question: when users ask AI chatbots about climate change, do these tools provide accurate information, repeat misleading claims, or subtly influence users’ views? This PhD project will examine both the content produced by AI systems and the effect that content has on human users. Research Focus: Generative AI, Climate Beliefs, and Climate-Related Behaviour The project will study how AI-generated responses shape public understanding of climate change. It will examine whether AI chatbots reinforce climate misinformation, correct misleading claims, or influence people’s willingness to take pro-environmental action. The research will look at two broad areas: The content and accuracy of information provided by generative AI chatbots The downstream effect of AI-generated content on people’s beliefs and behaviours This makes the studentship highly relevant to current debates around artificial intelligence, information integrity, climate communication, digital trust, misinformation, and public engagement with science. Project Objective One: How Do Large Language Models Respond to Climate Change Queries? The first major objective of the PhD project is to investigate how different large language models respond to user queries about climate change. The successful candidate will examine whether AI chatbots provide accurate answers, agree with misleading statements, or become vulnerable to leading questions during naturalistic conversations. For example, the project may explore how chatbots respond to claims such as: “The climate has always been changing, so how can humans have had anything to do with it?” “Isn’t climate change just part of a natural cycle?” “Do scientists really agree that humans are causing climate change?” These types of questions are important because they resemble real conversations that users may have with AI systems. The research will assess whether AI systems challenge misleading assumptions, provide balanced scientific explanations, or unintentionally validate inaccurate claims. The project will also investigate whether climate-related misinformation may have been included in the training data of large language models and whether this affects the responses generated by the systems. Project Objective Two: How Does AI-Generated Climate Information Affect Human Beliefs and Behaviour? The second major objective is to measure the impact of AI-generated responses on human participants. Participants will be asked to engage in a conversation about climate change with an “AI chatbot.” During the experiment, they will receive answers that vary in accuracy and bias. The researcher will then examine how this exposure affects participants’ beliefs about climate change and their willingness to engage in climate-friendly behaviour. The project will focus on questions such as: Does AI-generated misinformation change people’s belief in anthropogenic climate change? Are people more likely to trust misinformation when it appears to come from an AI chatbot? Is AI-generated misinformation more persuasive than misinformation shared through social media posts or plain text? Can subtle changes in chatbot responses encourage greener behaviour? How do users respond when an AI system presents climate information with different levels of bias or accuracy? This part of the research will contribute to a better understanding of how AI systems influence public opinion and behavioural choices in relation to climate change. Comparing AI Chatbots with Other Forms of Misinformation Delivery A key strength of this PhD project is that it will compare AI-generated misinformation with other forms of misinformation delivery. The project will examine whether misinformation from AI chatbots is more persuasive than misinformation presented through: Social media posts Plain text information Other non-interactive formats This comparison is important because AI chatbots are interactive. Unlike a static post or article, a chatbot responds directly to the user, adapts to their questions, and may appear conversational, helpful, or authoritative. This interactive format could make AI-generated misinformation especially influential. By comparing AI-based misinformation with other formats, the research will help clarify whether chatbot-generated content carries unique persuasive power. Investigating Behavioural Nudges Toward Greener Choices The project will also investigate whether subtle changes in chatbot responses can encourage users toward more environmentally friendly behaviours. This part of the research is especially important because generative AI may not only spread misinformation; it may also be used to support better public understanding and positive behavioural change. The research may explore how chatbot responses can be designed to: Correct misleading claims about climate change Improve understanding of human-caused climate change Encourage pro-environmental decisions Support climate literacy Reduce the influence of misinformation Promote greener behaviours through carefully designed messaging This gives the PhD project both a critical and constructive dimension. It examines the risks of AI-generated misinformation while also exploring how AI tools might be used responsibly to support climate action. Structured Cohort-Based PhD Training Programme The successful applicant will join a structured cohort-based PhD training programme. This means that, in addition to conducting individual PhD research, the student will be part of a wider learning and research community. The programme includes transferable skills training, group-based activities, domain-specific learning, peer-led opportunities, and career development support. Training and Development Opportunities The successful applicant will benefit from a wide range of training and development activities, including: Transferable Skills Training The programme will provide training in areas such as: Communication skills Ethics Entrepreneurship Research presentation Professional development Academic and non-academic career preparation These skills will help the PhD student communicate research findings clearly, work across disciplines, and prepare for future roles in academia, policy, industry, data science, AI governance, or research communication. Group Activities The student will also participate in collaborative activities such as: Hackathons Spin-out sprints Interdisciplinary group projects Team-based problem-solving activities These activities are designed to encourage creativity, innovation, collaboration, and practical application of research ideas. Domain-Specific Training The programme will provide training on specialist topics relevant to the student’s research area. These may include subjects connected to: Artificial intelligence Data science Psychology Climate misinformation Large language models Information integrity Behavioural science Quantitative research methods Experimental design Peer-Led Learning Opportunities The student will also benefit from peer-led learning, including: Research seminars Peer-designed workshops Reading groups Collaborative discussions Knowledge-sharing sessions These activities will allow the student to learn from other researchers, share ideas, and develop within a supportive academic community. Career and Skills Development Dedicated career and skills development activities will also be offered. These activities are intended to support the student’s long-term professional growth and prepare them for opportunities after the PhD. Funding Package The studentship provides a generous funding package for four years. The funding includes: Annual stipend: €25,000 per year Duration: Four years Tax status: Tax-free stipend Additional support: Funding available for conference attendance and training activities Fees: EU student fees are covered automatically Non-EU fees: Non-EU students may receive a fee waiver from UCD This funding structure gives the successful applicant financial support throughout the full duration of the PhD programme. The additional funds for conferences and training activities will also help the student build networks, present research, and access further professional development opportunities. Application Process Applications must be submitted through the general cohort-based programme application form. Applicants interested in this specific PhD project should follow these instructions carefully: Use the general cohort-based programme application form. Select “Information Integrity” as the first choice theme. Select UCD as the first choice institution. Specify the particular project on Generative AI and Climate Change Misinformation . Applicants are strongly encouraged to contact the primary research supervisor before applying. The primary research supervisor is: Prof. Ciara Greene Email: ciara.greene@ucd.ie Contacting the supervisor before applying is strongly encouraged because it allows applicants to introduce their background, ask project-specific questions, and confirm that their research interests align with the PhD topic. Application Deadline Applications must be submitted by: 5:00pm on 2 June 2026 Interviews will be held later in June 2026, with decisions expected soon after. Applicants should prepare their materials early and avoid waiting until the final day, especially because this is a competitive funded PhD opportunity with a fixed start date in September 2026. Ideal Candidate Profile The ideal candidate will have a strong academic background, excellent quantitative skills, strong communication abilities, and an interest in the intersection of psychology, artificial intelligence, misinformation, and climate change. The project is especially suitable for applicants who enjoy interdisciplinary research and are interested in both technical and human aspects of AI systems. Mandatory Requirements Applicants must meet the following mandatory criteria: An honours degree in Psychology or a cognate discipline At least upper second class honours, or international equivalent Excellent skills in quantitative data analysis Excellent communication skills, including oral, written, and presentation skills Excellent organisational and administrative skills Proven ability to work to deadlines These requirements reflect the research demands of the project. The successful applicant will need to design studies, analyse data, communicate findings, work independently, and manage a multi-year research project. Desirable Criteria The following criteria are desirable but not mandatory: A Master’s degree in Psychology, Cognitive Science, or a cognate discipline Background in cognitive psychology Experience designing and running experiments Experience running online experiments Strong computer programming skills Experience working with large language models Applicants who meet some or all of the desirable criteria may be especially well-positioned for the project, particularly if they can demonstrate strong research potential and interest in generative AI, misinformation, and climate change. Why This PhD Opportunity Matters This PhD studentship addresses a timely and important global issue. Climate change misinformation continues to shape public debate, policy attitudes, and individual behaviour. At the same time, generative AI tools are becoming major sources of information for millions of users. Understanding how these tools respond to climate-related misinformation is essential for improving information integrity and responsible AI design. The project is important because it will help answer questions such as: Can generative AI systems spread climate misinformation? How accurate are chatbot responses to climate-related questions? Are AI-generated responses more persuasive than traditional misinformation formats? How do users’ beliefs change after interacting with AI-generated climate content? Can AI systems be designed to encourage more accurate understanding and greener behaviours? The findings could be valuable for researchers, policymakers, AI developers, climate communicators, educators, and organisations working on digital trust and information integrity. Key Benefits of the Studentship This funded PhD opportunity offers several major benefits: Four years of funded doctoral research Tax-free annual stipend of €25,000 Additional funding for conferences and training EU student fees covered automatically Possible fee waiver for non-EU students Supervision from experts in psychology, mathematics, and statistics Opportunity to work on a highly relevant AI and climate misinformation topic Structured cohort-based PhD training Transferable skills development Peer-led learning and research community support Career and professional development activities Interdisciplinary training in data science, AI, psychology, and information integrity How to Apply Interested applicants should apply through the general cohort-based programme application form and select: Theme: Information Integrity Institution: UCD Project: Generative AI and Climate Change Misinformation Applicants are strongly encouraged to contact Prof. Ciara Greene at ciara.greene@ucd.ie before submitting their application. The deadline for applications is 5:00pm on 2 June 2026 . Conclusion The Funded PhD Studentship in Generative AI and Climate Change Misinformation at UCD is an excellent opportunity for candidates interested in psychology, artificial intelligence, climate communication, misinformation, behavioural science, and data science. With a four-year funding package, structured training, expert supervision, and a research topic of major global relevance, this studentship offers a strong pathway for applicants who want to contribute to responsible AI research and the fight against climate misinformation. The successful candidate will help generate new knowledge about how generative AI systems influence public understanding of climate change and how AI tools might be designed to support more accurate information and greener behaviours. Application deadline: 5:00 pm on 2 June 2026. APPLY HERE. For more information about this opportunity click here. Discover more scholarship opportunities on OFY click here. The post Funded PhD Studentship in Generative AI and Climate Change Misinformation at UCD 2026 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: World Bank Manage Successful Field Research Course 2026: Free Online Certification Training for Researchers and Development Professionals
summary: World Bank Group is inviting researchers, development practitioners, graduate students, and public officials from around the world to enroll in the Manage Successful Field Research: New Edition – Spring 2026 online training programme offered through the World Bank Group Data Academy. This highly valuable e-learning course is specifically designed to help professionals strengthen their field research implementation skills, improve data collection quality, understand research ethics, and explore modern technologies including AI tools for field research. Participants who successfully complete the programme will receive a free certificate , making this an excellent opportunity for individuals seeking to enhance their academic, professional, and development-sector credentials. The programme combines practical lessons, expert guidance, assessments, discussion forums, and real-world case studies to provide participants with comprehensive field research management skills. About the World Bank Group Data Academy The World Bank Group Data Academy is a learning platform developed to equip researchers, policymakers, analysts, and development professionals with practical tools and modern methodologies for evidence-based decision-making. The academy provides training programmes focused on: Field research Monitoring and evaluation Data analytics Research implementation Policy evaluation Development impact analysis Its courses are led by experienced experts from the World Bank’s Development Impact Department (DIME Analytics), helping learners gain direct exposure to globally recognized research standards and best practices. What Is the “Manage Successful Field Research” Course? The Manage Successful Field Research course is a virtual training programme that teaches participants how to successfully plan, implement, monitor, and manage high-quality field research projects. The course focuses on every stage of field research, including: Research preparation Survey management Data collection Ethical considerations Monitoring implementation Data quality assurance Emerging technologies and AI tools Sharing and communicating research findings The programme also gives learners access to a global network of field researchers and development experts. Course Duration and Format Training Format Fully online Self-paced e-learning Course Availability Period Course materials will be accessible from: 12 May 2026 to 11 September 2026 Estimated Learning Hours 30 hours total duration 5 hours of video content Learning Features Participants will gain access to: Video lessons Reading materials Assessments Discussion forums Reflection activities Certification upon completion Why This Course Matters Field research is essential in development work, policy analysis, monitoring and evaluation, public health, economics, and social sciences. However, conducting successful field research requires technical expertise, strong planning, ethical awareness, and quality assurance systems. This course helps learners build those competencies while exposing them to modern research innovations and practical implementation techniques. Participants will learn how to avoid common research challenges, improve data reliability, and manage research projects more effectively. Key Learning Objectives By the end of the programme, participants will be able to: 1. Develop High-Quality Field Research Projects Learners will understand how to: Structure effective research projects Design research methodologies Create implementation plans Develop strong data collection systems 2. Ensure Data Quality and Research Integrity The programme emphasizes: Ethical research standards Data privacy protection Quality control systems Monitoring implementation fidelity Real-time data verification 3. Use Modern Tools for Research Participants will explore: Emerging AI tools for field research Non-traditional data sources Administrative datasets Secondary data processing techniques 4. Improve Survey Management Skills The course teaches how to: Design surveys Pilot questionnaires Engage with data collectors Work with research firms Improve measurement accuracy 5. Strengthen Monitoring and Evaluation Expertise Participants involved in monitoring and evaluation will gain practical knowledge in: Tracking implementation quality Measuring programme impact Managing field operations Ensuring reliable research outputs Detailed Breakdown of Course Modules Module 1: Welcome Introduction This introductory section includes: Course orientation Pre-course assessment Learner expectations Opening keynote session Introduction forums Participants are introduced to the importance of research in transforming development outcomes. Module 2: Design Impactful Field Research This module covers: Experimental and non-experimental methods Establishing causality Sampling techniques Ethical research practices Data privacy AI tools for field research This section is especially useful for learners involved in impact evaluation and evidence-based policymaking. Module 3: Manage Successful Surveys Participants learn how to: Design and pilot surveys Coordinate data collection teams Improve measurement quality Avoid survey errors Work with external firms This module focuses heavily on practical implementation challenges. Module 4: Go Beyond Surveys This advanced module explores: Non-traditional data sources Administrative data Secondary datasets Mixed-methods research Participants gain exposure to modern approaches that extend beyond traditional surveys. Module 5: Ensure Quality This section focuses on maintaining research integrity through: Real-time data quality checks Field validation Monitoring implementation fidelity Quality assurance systems This module is particularly valuable for monitoring and evaluation professionals. Module 6: Closing Session The final section discusses: Sharing research findings Research communication Post-course assessments Final reflections Certification requirements Free Certificate Upon Completion One of the most attractive aspects of this programme is that participants who complete the course requirements will receive a free certificate . This certificate can strengthen: CVs and resumes Academic portfolios Research credentials Development-sector applications Monitoring and evaluation career opportunities Meet the Trainers The course is delivered by experts from DIME Analytics and the World Bank Group. Featured Trainers Maria Jones Senior Economist Team Lead, DIME Analytics Roshni Khincha Research Analyst Data Coordinator, DIME Analytics The programme also includes contributions from the wider DIME Analytics Team. Benefits of Joining This Programme Participants will benefit from: Professional Development Strengthen research implementation skills Improve analytical capacity Learn international best practices Global Networking Connect with researchers worldwide Participate in discussion forums Interact with development experts Career Advancement This training is highly valuable for careers in: International development Public policy Research institutions NGOs Monitoring and evaluation Academia Flexible Learning Because the course is online and self-paced, participants can learn conveniently from anywhere in the world. Who Should Apply? This programme is ideal for: Development practitioners Young researchers Graduate students Monitoring and evaluation professionals Public servants Policy analysts Academics Data professionals Application Deadline Enrollment is currently open. Course materials will remain accessible until: 11 September 2026 Interested applicants are encouraged to apply as early as possible before the course enrollment closes. Click here for more and to apply For more global scholarship opportunities click here . title: Uppsala University Research Assistant Jobs 2026 in Sweden | Fully Funded Peace and Conflict Research Opportunity
summary: The Department of Peace and Conflict Research at Uppsala University is currently inviting applications for two full-time Research Assistant positions under an exciting international research project titled “Why Evictions Turn Violent: The Politics of Growing Cities.” The opportunity is based in Uppsala, Sweden, and offers selected candidates the chance to work closely with leading researchers on conflict, urban violence, and forced evictions in Ethiopia and Kenya. The six-month employment opportunity will begin on 1 September 2026 or as agreed, and applications are open until 5 June 2026. Applicants interested in peacebuilding, urban governance, development studies, African politics, conflict analysis, and data-driven research should strongly consider applying for this opportunity. About Uppsala University Uppsala University is one of Europe’s oldest and most prestigious research institutions. The university is internationally recognized for excellence in education and research and currently hosts over 53,000 students and 7,600 employees. The university’s Department of Peace and Conflict Research was established in 1971 and has become a globally respected center for peace research and conflict studies. Every year, the department enrolls around 1,000 students across undergraduate, postgraduate, and doctoral programs. The department focuses on producing impactful research that contributes to global peace, conflict prevention, and social transformation. About the Research Project The project “Why Evictions Turn Violent: The Politics of Growing Cities” is led by researcher Emma Elfversson and funded by the Swedish Research Council. The project officially began in January 2026 and will continue through 2029. Its primary objective is to investigate why forced evictions in rapidly growing cities sometimes escalate into violence. Researchers will conduct: Large-scale quantitative analyses of forced eviction data Comparative urban conflict studies Case studies from Ethiopia and Kenya Detailed examination of eviction-related violence dynamics The project specifically focuses on ten major and secondary cities across the two countries, where forced evictions have become increasingly significant political and social issues. What Will the Research Assistants Do? Selected applicants will primarily support the project through extensive data collection and research activities. Key responsibilities include: Collecting and coding fine-grained data related to forced evictions Analyzing secondary sources and reports Working with urban conflict and violence datasets Supporting administrative and project management tasks Assisting with workshops and project coordination Participating in writing and documentation activities Collaborating with project researchers and fellow research assistants The work will be conducted onsite in Uppsala, Sweden, and the assistants will report directly to the project leader. Required Qualifications To qualify for the research assistant positions, applicants must possess: A Bachelor’s degree Strong academic knowledge in peace and conflict studies, development studies, or another relevant discipline Basic software proficiency, especially in Microsoft Excel Excellent spoken and written English skills Ability to work independently and collaboratively Strong analytical, organizational, and problem-solving skills High attention to detail and accuracy Preferred Qualifications Although not mandatory, the following qualifications will significantly strengthen an application: Fluency or advanced knowledge of Amharic, Kiswahili, or another Ethiopian language Master’s-level studies in peace and conflict or related fields Previous experience with conflict-related data collection or coding Experience researching Ethiopia and/or Kenya Knowledge of GIS analysis Familiarity with statistical software such as R The project places high value on applicants who can work with local-language sources from the study regions. Employment Details Here are the key employment details for the opportunity: Position: Research Assistant Number of Positions: 2 Employment Type: Temporary Full-Time Duration: 6 Months Start Date: 1 September 2026 or as agreed Latest Possible Start Date: 1 October 2026 Location: Uppsala, Sweden Working Hours: 100% Salary: Individual salary arrangement The position may also require security vetting before employment is finalized. Application Requirements Applicants must submit the following documents: Responses to the two mandatory application questions A detailed CV Clear description of language skills A relevant academic writing sample such as a thesis or research paper Applicants are encouraged to clearly highlight: Research experience Data analysis skills Language abilities Regional expertise related to Ethiopia or Kenya Application Deadline The deadline to apply for the Uppsala University Research Assistant positions is: 5 June 2026 Applicants should ensure all materials are submitted before the deadline through the university’s recruitment portal. How to Apply Applications must be submitted through the official recruitment system of Uppsala University Why You Should Apply This opportunity offers young researchers and graduates a rare chance to gain hands-on international research experience in one of Europe’s leading peace research institutions. Benefits include: International research exposure Professional experience in conflict and urban studies Collaboration with globally recognized researchers Advanced data collection and analysis experience Opportunity to work in Sweden Networking opportunities in academia and policy research Strong career development for future postgraduate or research pathways Deadline: 5th June,2026. APPLY HERE For more information on this opportunity, click here. For more global opportunities, click here.
